label: paragraph_title
content: ## Updates in Version 3.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia include:

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-9

label: algorithm
content:
- Therapy For Relapsed/Refractory Disease
  - Targeted therapy: Therapy for AML with NPM1 mutation, ziftomenib added.
- Principles of Systemic Therapy For AML; Therapy For Relapsed/Refractory Disease: Targeted therapy
  - Therapy added: Ziftomenib (NPM1 mutation)
  - Regimen added: 600 mg once daily on days 1-28 of a 28-day cycle
- Footnote v modified: Revumenib is FDA approved for the treatment of relapsed or refractory acute leukemia with a KMT2A translocation, as well as relapsed or refractory AML with a susceptible NPM1 mutation with no satisfactory alternative treatment options, in adult and pediatric patients $\geq1$ year.
- Footnote x added: Ziftomenib is FDA approved for the treatment of adults with relapsed or refractory AML with a susceptible NPM1 mutation with no satisfactory alternative treatment options.
- Reference 64 added: Wang ES, Montesinos P, Foran J, et al. Ziftomenib in relapsed or refractory NPM1-mutated AML. J Clin Oncol 2025;43:3381-3390.
- MS-1
- The discussion section has been updated to reflect the changes in the algorithm.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Updates in Version 2.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia include:

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-E 9 OF 14

label: text
content: - Fludarabine + cytarabine + G-CSF + idarubicin + venetoclax regimen modified: Fludarabine 30 mg/m $^2$ on days 2-6; cytarabine 1.5-2 g/m $^2$ over 4 hours starting 4 hours after fludarabine infusion on days 2-6; G-CSF SC daily on days 1-7; idarubicin 6 mg/m $^2$ IV on days 4-5; venetoclax 400 mg on days 1-7 44. Corresponding reference updated to DiNardo CD, Jen WY, Takahashi K, et al. Long term results of venetoclax combined with FLAG-IDA induction and consolidation for newly diagnosed and relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia. Leukemia 2025;39:854-863.

label: text
content: - The discussion section has been updated to reflect the changes in the algorithm.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia include:

label: paragraph_title
content: ## General

label: text
content: - References updated across the guidelines.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## EVAL-1

label: text
content: - Evaluation For AML

label: text
content: 5th bullet modified: Bone marrow (BM) core biopsy and aspirate analyses, including immunophenotyping by immunohistochemistry (IHC) stains + flow cytometry, and the analysis of chromosomal structural variations by cytogenetics, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), and or whole genome next generation sequencing (NGS) (AML-A)

label: text
content: 6th bullet modified: Molecular analyses for lesions that allow risk stratification as per ELN 2022 (ASXL1, BCOR, KIT, EZH2, FLT3 [internal tandem duplication (ITD) and tyrosine kinase domain (TKD)] , NPM1, in-frame bZIP mutation in CEBPA, IDH1, IDH2, RUNX1, SF3B1, SRSF2, STAG2, TP53, U2AF1, ZRSR2 and other mutations) (AML-A)

label: text
content: 8th bullet modified: Consider additional molecular and genetic testing for heritable hematologic malignancy predisposition in a subset of patients, particularly in patients <50 years and those with a family history (see MDS-D and MDS-E in the NCCN Guidelines for Myelodysplastic Syndromes)

label: text
content: 13th bullet modified: Lumbar puncture (LP), if symptomatic (category 2B for asymptomatic) (AML-B)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia include:

label: paragraph_title
content: ## EVAL-2

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Diagnosis

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML

label: text
content: 1st bullet modified: For patients with hyperleukocytosis uncontrolled with hydroxyurea or leukapheresis, one dose of cytarabine (0.54-2 g) may be considered prior to receiving diagnostic results. Leukapheresis may be considered for certain patients with symptomatic hyperleukocytosis, though data for benefit are limited. EVAL-2A

label: text
content: - Footnote a added: Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A).

label: paragraph_title
content: ## APL-3

label: text
content: - APL Treatment Induction (High Risk)

label: text
content: Preferred Regimens pathway modified: BM aspirate and biopsy at day 28 to document remission consider LP before proceeding with consolidation (Also Useful in Certain Circumstances on APL-3 and Prolonged QTcF on APL-4)

label: text
content: Footnote g added: Begin prophylaxis with prednisone; the optimal duration of steroid prophylaxis is unknown. If differentiation syndrome develops, change to dexamethasone. Lo-Coco F, et al. N Engl J Med 2013;369:111-121. See Principles of Supportive Care for APL (APL-A). (Also APL-4)

label: text
content: Footnote m added: For all patients with high-risk APL, consider LP before proceeding with consolidation. (Also APL-4)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## APL-5

label: text
content: - Footnote n modified: PCR should be performed on a blood sample at completion of consolidation to document molecular remission. In patients receiving the ATRA/ arsenic regimen, consider earlier sampling at 3-4 months during consolidation. Prior practice guidelines have recommended monitoring blood by PCR every 3 mo for 2 y to detect molecular relapse. We continue to endorse this for patients with high-risk disease or those >60 y of age or who had long interruptions during consolidation, or patients on regimens that use maintenance and are not able to tolerate maintenance. Clinical experience indicates that risk of relapse in patients with low-risk disease who are in molecular remission at completion of consolidation is low and monitoring may not be necessary outside the setting of a clinical trial. While long-term monitoring has been standard, with newer, more effective regimens, the value is less certain.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## APL-A

label: text
content: - Principles of Supportive Care For APL

label: text
content: > 5th bullet, 2nd sub bullet modified: For patients at high risk (WBC count >10 x $1 0^{9} / \mathrm{L}$ ) for developing differentiation syndrome and for those receiving an ATRA/arsenic trioxide-based regimen, initiate prophylaxis with corticosteroids, either prednisone 0.5 mg/kg day 1 or dexamethasone 10 mg every 12 hours (see NCCN Guidelines for Prevention and Treatment of Cancer-Related Infections). Taper the steroid dose over a period of several days. If patient develops differentiation syndrome, change prednisone to dexamethasone 10 mg every 12 hours until count recovery or risk of differentiation has abated.

label: text
content: > 6th bullet, 1st sub bullet, 2nd sub sub bullet modified: Serum electrolytes (Ca, K, Mg, phosphorus) and creatinine

label: paragraph_title
content: ## APL-B1OF6

label: text
content: - Principles of Systemic Therapy For APL

label: text
content: Footnote b added: The optimal dosing of arsenic trioxide for patients with obesity is unknown, though retrospective studies suggest there may be a role for dose capping. Zacholski K, et al. J Oncol Pharm Pract 2022;28:1340-1349; Jen WY, et al. Leuk Lymphoma 2024;65:378-382. (Also APL-B 2 of 6, APL-B 3 of 6, APL-B 4A of 6, APL-B 5 of 6)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-1

label: text
content: - Intensive Induction Eligible

label: text
content: 1st pathway, Favorable-risk AML by cytogenetics (core binding factor [CBF]-AML)

label: text
content: Other Recommended, regimen removed: 7+3 (mitoxantrone) (for age $\geq60$ y)

label: text
content: 2nd pathway, Favorable-risk AML by molecular mutation profiled or Intermediate-risk AML per ELN (AML-A)

label: text
content: $\diamond$ Preferred, regimen removed: 7+3 (mitoxantrone) (for age $\geq60$ y)

label: text
content: Continued

label: text
content: Other Recommended, 4th regimen modified: CLAG-M (cladribine + cytarabine + G-CSF - mitoxantrone) (category 2B; use with caution in patients >60 y)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia include:

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-2

label: text
content: - Intensive Induction Eligible

label: text
content: Poor/Adverse-Risk Groups

label: text
content: 1st pathway

label: text
content:
- 3rd bullet modified: Cytogenetic or molecular changes consistent with MDS (previously classified as AML with myelodysplasia-related changes [AML-MRC])

- Intensive Induction Eligible

label: text
content: Principles of Venetoclax, see AML-J removed

label: text
content: 1st pathway, Therapy-related AML other than CBF-AML; Antecedent MDS/chronic myelomonocytic leukemia (CMML); Cytogenetic or molecular changes consistent with MDS (previously classified as AML with myelodysplasia-related changes [AML-MRC]), Other recommended

label: text
content: - 5th regimen added: CLIA (cladribine + idarubicin + cytarabine) + venetoclax (category 2B)

label: text
content: - 6th regimen added: FLAG-IDA + venetoclax (category 2B; use with caution in patients >60 y)

label: text
content: - $\diamond$ 2nd pathway, Poor-risk AML without TP53 mutation or del(17p) abnormality. Other recommended - 7th regimen added: CLIA + venetoclax (category 2B)

label: text
content: - 8th regimen added: FLAG-IDA + venetoclax (category 2B; use with caution in patients >60 y)

label: text
content: - 2nd pathway, Poor-risk AML without TP53 mutation or del(17p) abnormality, Useful in certain circumstances

label: text
content: - Regimen removed: 7 + 3 (mitoxantrone) (for age $\geq60$ y ) (category 2B)

label: text
content: - Regimen removed: Cytarabine + (daunorubicin or idarubicin) + etoposide (category 2B for patients >45 y) (use with caution in patients >60 y)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-2A

label: text
content: - Footnote k modified: For CBF-AML with FLT3-TKD mutation, the Panel prefers gemtuzumab ozogamicin over an FLT3 inhibitor. For CBF-AML with FLT3-ITD, there is insufficient data to recommend one as preferred over the other.

label: text
content: - Footnote r modified: There are limited data supporting the use of this regimen in patients aged <60 years. For patients with AML with cytogenetic changes consistent with MDS (previously classified as AML-MRC) and previous hypomethylating agent (HMA) exposure, the benefit from standard induction did not differ from the benefit with CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cytarabine and daunorubicin. Lancet JE, et al. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:2684-2692. While the mutational definition of AML-MRC as it applies to the use of CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal cytarabine and daunorubicin was not studied in the original trial, its use can be considered. There is emerging data that CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cytarabine and daunorubicin provides most benefit for patients with AML with mutations in SRSF2, SF3B1, EZH2, U2AF1, ZRSR2, BCOR, STAG2, or ASXL1 (Shimony SO, et al. Blood 2024;144:60). (Also AML-6A)

label: text
content: - Footnote u modified: Patients with cytopenias with disease in remission should take breaks between cycles. For more details about cycle length, see Principles of Venetoclax (AML-J). (Also AML-4A)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-3

label: text
content: - Follow-Up And Reinduction After Cytarabine-Based Induction

label: text
content: 3rd column

label: text
content: $$\diamond $Regimen removed: 7+3 (mitoxantrone) (for age$ \geq60 $ y)

label: text
content: 6th regimen added: HMA (azacitidine or decitabine) + venetoclax

label: text
content: 7th bullet modified: CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cytarabine and daunorubicin (therapy-related AML other than CBFAML, antecedent MDS/ CMML, or cytogenetic or molecular changes consistent with MDS AML-MRG) (preferred only if given in induction; BM aspirate and biopsy 14-21 days after start of therapy)

label: text
content: 8th bullet modified: Consider regimens for relapsed/refractory disease, including targeted therapies, if lack of response to induction (Response criteria, see AML-I). Therapy for Relapsed/Refractory Disease (AML-9)

label: text
content: 5th column, 1st pathway modified: Response GR (Screening LP, see AML-B) (Response criteria, see AML-I)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## 6th column

label: text
content: 1st pathway modified: Consolidation (if received intensive re-induction Eligible) (AML-6)

label: text
content: 2nd pathway added: Continue lower intensity therapy (if received HMA+ venetoclax reinduction) (AML-5)

label: text
content: Continued

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia include:

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-3A

label: text
content: - Footnote s added: Venetoclax combination regimens may be continued for patients whose disease demonstrates clinical improvement (CR/CR with incomplete hematologic recovery [CRi]), with consideration of subsequent transplant, where appropriate. DiNardo CD, et al. Lancet Oncol 2018;19:216-228; Wei A, et al. Blood 2017;130:890; DiNardo CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17; DiNardo CD, et al. N Engl J Med 2020;383:617-629; Kadia TM, et al. J Clin Oncol 2022;40:3848-3857.

label: text
content: - Footnote t added: Patients whose disease has progressed to AML from MDS after significant exposure to HMAs (ie, azacitidine, decitabine) may be less likely to derive benefit from continued treatment with HMAs compared to patients who are HMA-naive. Alternative treatment strategies should be considered. DiNardo CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17.

label: text
content: - Footnote u added: Patients with cytopenias with disease in remission should take breaks between cycles. For more details about cycle length, see Principles of Venetoclax (AML-J).

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-4

label: text
content: - Lower Intensity Therapy (Intensive Induction Ineligible or Declines)

label: text
content: Treatment Induction, AML with IDH1 mutation

label: text
content: Preferred

label: text
content: - Regimen moved here from Other Recommended: Decitabine + venetoclax

label: text
content: - Useful in Certain Circumstances

label: text
content: - 3rd regimen added: Olutasidenib (category 2B) (not eligible for preferred regimen and not eligible for ivosidenib due to prolonged QTcF)

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-4A

label: text
content: - Footnote cc modified: Response to treatment with IDH inhibitors enasidenib or ivosidenib may take 3-5 months.

label: text
content: - Footnote dd modified: IDH inhibitors Enasidenib or ivosidenib increases the risk for differentiation syndrome and hyperleukocytosis that may require treatment with hydroxyurea and steroids. Monitor closely for differentiation syndrome and initiate therapy to resolve symptoms according to indications. Note that differentiation syndrome can occur later (up to several months after induction).

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-8

label: text
content: - AML Surveillance And Therapy For Relapsed/Refractory Disease (After Completion Of Consolidation)

label: text
content: 1st column, 4th bullet modified: Confirm molecular remission and monitor for molecular relapse, if applicable. See Measurable (Minimal) Residual Disease Assessment (AML-H)

label: text
content: 4th column, 2nd option modified: Targeted therapy alone or in combination (AML-9) followed by allogeneic HCT

label: text
content: - Footnote ww modified: Molecular and cytogenetic analyses Multi-gene molecular profiling/targeted NGS (including testing for IDH1/IDH2, FLT3 mutations, and KMT2A rearrangements) are suggested as it may assist with selection of therapy and appropriate clinical trials (Discussion). Molecular testing should be repeated at each relapse or progression.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-9

label: text
content: - Therapy For Relapsed/Refractory Disease

label: text
content: - Targeted therapy: Therapy for AML with NPM1 mutation, revumenib added.

label: text
content: - Footnote zz added: There have been trials conducted combining targeted therapies with other agents.

label: text
content: - Footnote aaa added: Best sequencing of targeted therapies is unknown at this time and depends on clinical context.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-B

label: text
content: - Evaluation And Treatment of CNS Leukemia

label: text
content: Footnote c modified: Screening LP should be considered at first remission before first intensive consolidation for asymptomatic patients with monocytic differentiation, MPAL; WBC count >40 x 10 $^{9}$ /L at diagnosis, extramedullary disease, high-risk APL, intraparenchymal hemorrhage at diagnosis, or FLT3 mutations, MLLT3::KMT2A fusion, monocytic differentiation, or MPAL. For further information regarding MPAL, see NCCN Guidelines for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia.

$$

\diamond \mathrm {The CR rates and 2 - year OS in patients between 60 and 65 years of age treated with daunorubicin 90 mg/m^{2}} is also comparable to the outcome for idarubicin 12 mg/m^{2}; the higher-dose daunorubicin did not benefit patients >65 years of age (Löwenberg B, et al. N Engl J Med 2009;361:1235-1248).

$$

label: text
content: The use of doses of cytarabine $\geq2$ g/m $^{2}$ for induction outside the setting of a clinical trial is still controversial. While the remission rates are the same for doses of cytarabine >100 to 200 mg/m $^{2}$ and doses of cytarabine $\geq2$ g/m $^{2}$ , two studies have shown more rapid BM blast clearance after one cycle of high-dose therapy. Kern W, Estey EH. Cancer 2006;107:116-124.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia include:

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-H1OF4

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Measurable (Minimal) Residual Disease Assessment

label: text
content: The following bullets were moved from Methods of Testing section to General section:

label: text
content: There are commercially available tests that can be used for MRD assessments, as well as further testing methods at certain academic centers.

label: text
content: The most frequently used methods for MRD assessment include quantitative molecular assays such as real-time quantitative PCR (RQ-PCR) and multicolor flow cytometry (MFC) assays specifically designed to detect abnormal MRD immunophenotypes.

label: text
content: The optimal sample for initial MRD assessment is a first, dedicated pull of the BM aspirate. The quality of the sample is of paramount importance to have reliable evaluation. Once MRD-negative remission by BM is achieved, peripheral blood can be utilized for surveillance of MRD for PML:RAR alpha, NPM1, CBFB::MYH11, and RUNX1::RUNX1T1.

label: text
content: Mutations associated with clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) and aging (ie, DNMT3A, TET2, potentially ASXL1) are not considered reliable markers for MRD and are difficult to differentiate in routine practice.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-H1OF4

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Measurable (Minimal) Residual Disease Assessment

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Methods of Testing

label: text
content: Flow Cytometry, 1st bullet modified: If using flow cytometry to assess MRD, it is recommended that a specific MRD assay that incorporates both different from normal and leukemia-associated immunophenotypes (LAIPs) assessment is utilized, but, most importantly, that it is interpreted by an experienced hematopathologist, preferably at the same laboratory as diagnostic flow cytometry for consistency. Utilization of an assay with minimum limit of detection of $\leq10^{-3}$ is recommended.

label: text
content: Molecular, 1st bullet modified: The Panel recommends RQ-PCR for detection of PML::RAR alpha, NPM1, CBFB::MYH11, and RUNX1::RUNX1T1. Utilization of an assay with minimum limit of detection of $\leq10^{-4}$ is recommended.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-H2OF4

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Measurable (Minimal) Residual Disease Assessment

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Timing of MRD Assessment

label: text
content: $$\diamond$ 1st bullet, 3rd sub bullet modified: For NPM1-mutated, CBFB::MYH11, and RUNX1::RUNX1T1 AML: after two cycles of intensive chemotherapy (eg, one cycle of induction and one cycle of consolidation) and for serial monitoring every 6 to 12 weeks for 24 months.

label: text
content: 2nd bullet, 2nd sub bullet added: NPM1 molecular MRD is strongly prognostic in patients receiving venetoclax-based low-intensity therapy. The Panel supports monitoring NPM1 by RQ-PCR every 6 to 12 weeks in the BM or peripheral blood in patients receiving venetoclax-based low-intensity therapy.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## Management of MRD Positivity

label: text
content: 3rd bullet added: The Panel recommends post-transplant maintenance gilteritinib for patients with BM FLT3-ITD MRD $\geq10^{-6}$ by a highly sensitive, NGS-based targeted, deep-sequencing assay with a sensitivity level of $\leq10^{-5}$ pre- or post-allogeneic HCT.

label: text
content: 4th bullet added: As there is no clear optimal management of MRD positivity, the Panel favors a clinical trial for MRD positivity if available. If a clinical trial, allogeneic HCT, targeted therapy, or other consolidation strategies are not pursued, the Panel notes that venetoclax-based low-intensity therapy has demonstrated high rates of MRD reduction in MRD and oligoblastic AML.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## AML-I

label: text
content: - Response Criteria Definitions For Acute Myeloid Leukemia

label: text
content: 2nd bullet, 1st sub bullet modified: Morphologic CR - transfusion independence

label: text
content: 4th bullet, 1st sub bullet modified: Reappearance of leukemic blasts in the peripheral blood or the finding of $\geqg>5\%$ blasts in the BM, not attributable to another cause (eg, BM regeneration after consolidation therapy) or extramedullary relapse.

label: text
content: 5th bullet, 1st sub bullet modified: Inability to attain CR, CRh, or CRi following exposure to at least 2 courses of intensive induction therapy.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## 6th bullet added: MRD relapse

label: text
content: 1st sub bullet added: Conversion from MRD negativity to MRD positivity, independent of method of testing.

label: text
content: General, 5th bullet modified: Drug-drug interactions occur with strong or moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors and may require dose adjustment. Refer to venetoclax prescribing information (https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/cder/daf/index.cfm) and consult with a pharmacist for potential drug interactions. Strong or moderate CYP3A4 inducers (eg, carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampin) should be avoided.

label: paragraph_title

label: text
content: - Principles of BPDCN. General Principles:

label: text
content: 2nd bullet added: Diagnosis can be extremely challenging and whenever possible should involve an experienced hematopathologist.

label: text
content: 7th bullet modified: Prognosis for BPDCN is poor and the median OS is approximately 8-12 months when patients are treated with chemotherapy without consolidative allogeneic HCT.

label: text
content: 9th bullet modified: For patients who are fit, current treatment options for BPDCN include tagraxofusp-erzs and chemotherapy, whereas those with low albumin and/or comorbidities should receive non-intensive regimen options localized therapy or supportive care as shown in the algorithm (BPDCN-2).

label: paragraph_title
content: ## EVALUATION FOR AML $^{a}$

label: text
content: - History and physical (H&P)

label: text
content: - Complete blood count (CBC), platelets, differential, comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), uric acid, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)

label: text
content: - B12 and folic acid evaluation

label: text
content: - Prothrombin time (PT), partial thromboplastin time (PTT), fibrinogen

label: text
content: - Bone marrow (BM) core biopsy and aspirate analyses, including immunophenotyping by immunohistochemistry (IHC) stains + flow cytometry, and the analysis of chromosomal structural variations by cytogenetics, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), and next generation sequencing (NGS) (AML-A)

label: text
content: - Molecular analyses for lesions that allow risk stratification as per ELN 2022 $^{b}$ (AML-A)

label: text
content: - Comprehensive pathology report, including diagnosis of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) within the context of the diagnostic system used $^{1,2,3}$ blast count, cellularity, morphologic dysplasia, and mutation status if available

label: text
content: - Consider additional molecular and genetic testing for heritable hematologic malignancy predisposition in a subset of patients $^{c}$ (see MDS-D and MDS-E in the NCCN Guidelines for Myelodysplastic Syndromes)

label: text
content: - Early referral to transplant center and/or human leukocyte antigen (HLA) typing for patients with potential hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT) in the future (except for patients with a major contraindication to HCT)

label: text
content: - Brain CT without contrast, if central nervous system (CNS) hemorrhage suspected $^{d}$ (AML-B)

label: text
content: - Brain MRI with and without contrast, if leukemic meningitis suspected $^{d}$ (AML-B)

label: text
content: - Consider FDG-PET/CT in individuals with extramedullary disease

label: text
content: - Lumbar puncture (LP), if symptomatic $^{d}$ (AML-B)

label: text
content: - Evaluate myocardial function (echocardiogram or multigated acquisition [MUGA] scan) in patients with a history or symptoms of cardiac disease or prior/planned exposure to cardiotoxic drugs or radiation therapy (RT) to thorax

label: text
content: - Counsel patients on infertility risk, with consideration of fertility preservation as appropriate (see NCCN Guidelines for Adolescent and Young Adult (AYA) Oncology)

label: text
content: - Consider early integration of palliative care $^{4}$ (see NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care)

label: text
content: Multidisciplinary diagnostic studies (EVAL-2)

label: footer
content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

content: Footnotes and References on EVAL-2A

label: header
content: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion

label: image
content:
Diagnostic Studies

DIAGNOSIS $^{e,f,g,2,3}$
Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL):
In patients with clinical or pathologic features of APL, start all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) upon first suspicion of APL. Early initiation of ATRA may prevent the lethal complication of bleeding. If cytogenetic and molecular testing do not confirm APL, discontinue ATRA and continue treatment as for AML

AML:
To appropriately stratify available intensive therapy options, expedite test results of molecular and cytogenetic analyses for immediately actionable mutations or chromosomal abnormalities
• For patients with hyperleukocytosis uncontrolled with hydroxyurea, cytarabine (0.5–2 g) may be considered prior to receiving diagnostic results.
Leukapheresis may be considered for certain patients with symptomatic hyperleukocytosis, though data for benefit are limited. $^{5,6}$
• For patients who prefer not to receive blood transfusion(s), see AML-D for general considerations and supportive care
For suspicion of blastic plasmacytoid dendritic cell neoplasm (BPDCN), see BPDCN-INTRO

Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) See NCCN Guidelines for Myelodysplastic Syndromes

B-cell or T-cell lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma $^{f}$ See NCCN Guidelines for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia

label: footer
content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

label: footer
content: Footnotes and References on EVAL-2A

label: paragraph_title
content: ## FOOTNOTES FOR EVALUATION FOR AML

label: text
content:
a Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A).

b A variety of gene mutations are associated with specific prognoses (category 2A) and may guide medical decision-making (category 2B). Other genetic lesions may have therapeutic significance. The field of genomics in myeloid malignancies and related implications in AML are evolving rapidly. Mutations should be tested in all patients. Multiplex gene panels and targeted NGS analysis are recommended for the ongoing management of AML in various phases of treatment. (Papaemmanuil E, et al. N Engl J Med 2016;374:2209-2221; Lindsley RC, et al. Blood 2015;125:1367-1376; Dohner H, et al. Blood 2022;140:1345-1377; Gardin C, et al. Blood Adv 2020;4:1942-1949; Tazi Y, Arango-Ossa JE, et al. Nat Commun 2022;13:4622). (Discussion). If a test is not available at your institution, consult the pathology team (prior to performing the BM evaluation) about preserving material from the original diagnostic sample for future testing at an outside reference lab. Peripheral blood may alternatively be used to detect molecular abnormalities in patients with disease with morphologically detectable, circulating leukemic blasts.

label: text
content: $^{c}$ A heritable hematologic malignancy predisposition syndrome may account for cytopenias with or without MDS in some patients, whether presenting to pediatric or adult care centers (eg, GATA2 deficiency syndrome, Shwachman-Diamond syndrome, telomere biology disorders). Functional laboratory studies and constitutional (germline) genetic testing using large NGS panels to include genes listed on MDS-E in the NCCN Guidelines for Myelodysplastic Syndromes , whole exome or whole genome sequencing complemented within silico copy number variant (CNV) calling, and/or laboratory analysis for CNVs, such as microarray testing, is recommended for certain patients. See Genetic Familial High-Risk Assessment: Hereditary Myeloid Malignancy Predisposition Syndromes (MDS-D) and Gene Mutations Associated with Hereditary Myeloid Malignancy Predisposition Syndromes (MDS-E) in the NCCN Guidelines for Myelodysplastic Syndromes.

label: text
content: $^{d}$ Consider administration of one dose of intrathecal (IT) chemotherapy (methotrexate, cytarabine, or a combination of these agents) at time of diagnostic LP. See Evaluation and Treatment of CNS Leukemia (AML-B).

label: text
content: e At the moment, there are discrepancies between two recognized classification systems (Khoury JD, et al. Leukemia 2022;36:1703-1719; Arber DA, et al. Blood 2022;140:1200-1228) for AML. The NCCN Guidelines do not advocate for one over another. Providers should exercise their best clinical judgment related to these discrepancies, and the NCCN Panel recommends classification systems be written to allow for maximal clinical trial participation.

label: text
content: f When presented with rare cases such as acute leukemias of ambiguous lineage (ALAL), including mixed phenotype acute leukemias (MPAL) (according to 2022 WHO classification), consultation with an experienced hematopathologist is strongly recommended.

label: text
content: Patients with AML should preferably be cared for at experienced leukemia centers where clinical trials may be more available. Young adults may be eligible for pediatric trials.

label: text
content: h ATRA should be available in all community hospitals, so appropriate therapy can be started promptly.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## REFERENCES FOR EVALUATION FOR AML

label: reference
content: $\begin{array}{l} {{}^1\text{ Arber DA, Vardiman JW, Brunning RD, et al. Acute myeloid leukemia with recurrent genetic abnormalities. In: Swerdlow SH, Campo E, Harris NL, et al., eds. WHO Classification of Tumours of Haematopoietic and Lymphoid Tissues (ed 4th). Lyon: IARC; 2008:110-123.}} \\ {{}^2\text{ Khoury JD, Solary E, Abla O, et al. The 5th edition of the World Health Organization classification of haematolymphoid tumours: myeloid and histiocytic/dendritic neoplasms. Leukemia 2022;36:1703-1719.}} \\ {{}^3\text{ Arber DA, Orazi A, Hasserjian RP, et al. International Consensus Classification of myeloid neoplasms and acute leukemias: Integrating morphologic, clinical, and genomic data. Blood 2022;140:1200-1228.}} \\ {{}^4\text{ El-Jawahri A, LeBlanc TW, Kavanaugh A, et al. Effectiveness of integrated palliative and oncology care for patients with acute myeloid leukemia: a randomized clinical trial. JAMA Oncol 2021;7:238-245.}} \\ {{}^5\text{ Kim K, Konopleva M, DiNardo CD, et al. Urgent cytoreduction for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia patients allows acquisition of pretreatment genomic data and enrollment on investigational clinical trials. Am J Hematol 2022;97:885-894.}} \\ {{}^6\text{ Bewersdorf JP, Giri S, Tallman MS, et al. Leukapheresis for the management of hyperleukocytosis in acute myeloid leukemia-A systematic review and meta-analysis. Transfusion 2020;60:2360-2369.}} \end{array}$

label: text

label: text
content: APL CLASSIFICATION AND TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS

label: image
content:
The image is a flowchart that outlines the treatment and induction strategies for confirmed APL<sup>a,b</sup>. 

1. **Low risk (white blood cell [WBC] count ≤10 x 10^9/L)**
   - Treatment Induction (APL-2)

2. **High risk (WBC count >10 x 10^9/L)**
   - No cardiac issues
   - Treatment Induction (APL-3)
   - Cardiac issues (low ejection fraction [EF] or QTcF prolongation<sup>c</sup>)

label: footnote
content: a Therapy-related APL is treated the same as de novo APL. FLT3 inhibitors are not recommended for FLT3-positive APL. Gale RE, et al. Blood 2005;106:3768-3776.

label: footnote
content: $^{b}$ For patients with APL being treated at a community center, collaboration with a center with expertise has been shown to reduce induction mortality. Jillella AP, et al. JAMA Oncol 2025;11:400-407.

label: footnote
content: $^{c}$ Principles of Supportive Care for APL (APL-A).

label: footnote
content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

content: APL TREATMENT INDUCTION (LOW RISK) $^{b,c,d,e}$

label: paragraph_title
content: ## CONSOLIDATION THERAPY^{i,k}

label: text
content: See references (APL-7) and APL-B 1 of 6 for details on regimens

label: image
content: Markdown: Preferred RegimensATRA + daily arsenic trioxidef,g,1 (category 1)If blood count recovery by day 28 (platelet >100 x 10⁹/L, absolute neutrophil count [ANC] >1 x 10⁹/L), proceed with consolidation. BM aspirate and biopsy may be considered to document <5% blasts and no abnormal promyelocytesh,i,jContinue consolidaton as per protocol (see references and APL-B 1 of 6)ATRA + intermittent arsenic trioxidef,g,2 (category 1)Post-Consolidation Therapy (APL-5)Useful in Certain Circumstances (if arsenic is not available or contraindicated)ATRA + idarubicinf,g,3 (category 1)BM aspirate and biopsy days 28–35 to document <5% blasts and no abnormal promyelocytesi before proceeding with consolidationkATRA + gemtuzumab ozogamicinf,4Continue consolidaton as per protocol (see references and APL-B 1 of 6)

label: footnote
content: $^{b}$ For patients with APL being treated at a community center, collaboration with a center with expertise has been shown to reduce induction mortality. Jillella AP, et al. JAMA Oncol 2025;11:400-407.

label: vision_footnote
content: $^{c}$ Principles of Supportive Care for APL (APL-A).

label: footnote
content: $^{d}$ Several groups have published large trials with excellent outcomes. However, to achieve the expected results, one needs to use the regimen consistently through all components and not mix induction from one trial with consolidation from another.

label: footnote
content: $^{e}$ Early mortality is related to bleeding, differentiation syndrome, or infection. Persistent disease is rare.

label: vision_footnote
content: $^{f}$ Data suggest that lower doses of ATRA (25 mg/m $^{2}$ ) in divided doses until clinical remission may be used in children and adolescents. Kutny MA, et al. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:3021-3029.

label: vision_footnote
content: $^{9}$ Begin prophylaxis with prednisone; the optimal duration of steroid prophylaxis is unknown. If differentiation syndrome develops, change to dexamethasone. Lo-Coco F, et al. N Engl J Med 2013;369:111-121. See Principles of Supportive Care for APL (APL-A).

label: vision_footnote
content: $^{h}$ If no evidence of morphologic disease (<5% blasts and no abnormal promyelocytes), discontinue ATRA and arsenic trioxide to allow for peripheral blood recovery since arsenic trioxide can be associated with significant myelosuppression. If evidence of morphologic disease, continue ATRA and arsenic trioxide and repeat BM 1 week later.

label: vision_footnote
content: The presence of measurable cytogenetic and molecular markers post-induction does not carry prognostic or therapeutic implications.

label: vision_footnote
content: If full course of induction treatment is not given, or counts have not recovered by days 28-35, a BM aspirate and biopsy is recommended to document <5% blasts and no abnormal promyelocytes before proceeding with consolidation.

label: vision_footnote
content: k For regimens using high cumulative doses of cardiotoxic agents, consider reassessing cardiac function prior to each anthracycline/mitoxantrone-containing course.

label: vision_footnote
content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

label: header

label: image
content:
The image is a flowchart detailing the steps involved in a polymerase chain reaction (PCR) negative process after consolidation. The flowchart starts with "Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) negative" and leads to "Maintenance therapy if included in the initial treatment protocol." From there, it branches into "Monitor by PCRⁿ for up to 2 y after completion of treatment," which further divides into "PCR negative" and "PCR positive⁰." The next step is "Repeat PCRⁿ for confirmation within 4 wks," which also branches into "PCR negative" and "PCR positive⁰." Following this, the flowchart continues with "First relapse¹⁰ Therapy for Relapse (APL-6)."

This flowchart visually represents the sequential steps necessary to complete a PCR negative process after molecular remission, including maintenance therapy, monitoring, repeating PCR for confirmation, and the final relapse therapy.

label: vision_footnote
content: $^{n}$ PCR should be performed on a blood sample at completion of consolidation to document molecular remission. Prior practice guidelines have recommended monitoring blood by PCR every 3 mo for 2 y to detect molecular relapse. We continue to endorse this for patients with high-risk disease or those who had long interruptions during consolidation. Clinical experience indicates that risk of relapse in patients with low-risk disease who are in molecular remission at completion of consolidation is low and monitoring may not be necessary outside the setting of a clinical trial. While long-term monitoring has been standard, with newer, more effective regimens, the value is less certain.

label: vision_footnote
content: $^{0}$ To confirm PCR positivity, a second blood sample should be done in 2-4 weeks in a reliable laboratory. If molecular relapse is confirmed by a second positive test, treat as first relapse (APL-6). If the second test is negative, frequent monitoring (every 3 mo for 2 y) is strongly recommended to confirm that the test remains negative. The PCR testing lab should indicate the level of sensitivity of assay for positivity (most clinical labs have a sensitivity level of $1 0^{-4}$ ), and testing should be done in the same lab to maintain the same level of sensitivity. Consider consultation with a physician experienced in molecular diagnostics if results are equivocal.

label: vision_footnote
content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

content: References on APL-7

content: Markdown: See references (APL-7) and APL-B 5 of 6 for details on regimensFirst relapse (morphologic or molecular)ᵃEarly relapse (<6 mo) after ATRA and arsenic trioxide (no anthracycline)Anthracycline-based regimen as per APL-3q,r or gemtuzumab ozogamicin¹²,¹³No prior exposure to arsenic trioxide or early relapse (<6 mo) after ATRA + anthracycline-containing regimen¹¹Arsenic trioxide ± ATRA¹¹ ± gemtuzumab ozogamicinq,rLate relapse (≥6 mo) after arsenic trioxide-containing regimenArsenic trioxide ± ATRA ± (anthracycline or gemtuzumab ozogamicin)q,r,sSecond remission (morphologic)Consider CNS prophylaxis with intrathecal (IT) chemotherapy (methotrexate or cytarabine)PCR positive (by BM)Transplant candidateMatched sibling or alternative donor HCTᵇ,¹¹Not transplant candidateClinical trialClinical trial or Matched sibling or alternative donor HCTᵇ

label: text
content: $^{p}$ Document molecular panel to verify relapsed APL versus therapy-related AML. $^{q}$ Following the first cycle of consolidation, if the patient's disease is not in molecular remission (by quantitative PCR on BM sample), consider matched sibling or alternative donor (haploidentical, unrelated donor, or cord blood) HCT or clinical trial. Testing is recommended at least 2-3 weeks after the completion of arsenic trioxide to avoid false positives.

content: r Outcomes are uncertain in patients who received arsenic trioxide during initial induction/consolidation therapy.

label: footnote
content: $^{s}$ There is a small randomized trial that suggests that the addition of ATRA does not confer any benefit over arsenic trioxide alone. Raffoux E, et al. J Clin Oncol 2003;21:2326-2334.

content: $^{t}$ See NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## PRINCIPLES OF SUPPORTIVE CARE FOR APL $^{a}$

label: text
content: There are variations among institutions, but the following issues are important to consider in the management of APL.

label: text
content: - Early mortality is related to bleeding, differentiation syndrome, or infection.

label: text
content: - Clinical coagulopathy:

label: text
content: Management of clinical coagulopathy: Aggressive platelet transfusion support to maintain platelets $\geq50$ x $10^{9}$ /L; fibrinogen replacement with cryoprecipitate and fresh frozen plasma to maintain a level >150 mg/dL and PT and PTT close to normal values. Monitor daily until coagulopathy resolves.

label: text
content: Avoid use of tunneled catheter or port-a-cath.

label: text
content: - Leukapheresis $^{1}$ is not routinely recommended in patients with a high WBC count in APL because of the difference in leukemia biology; however, in life-threatening cases with leukostasis that is not responsive to other modalities, leukapheresis can be considered with caution.

label: text
content: - Hydroxyurea can be used to treat leukocytosis in individuals with low-risk disease who experience a rise in WBC count after treatment with an ATRA/arsenic trioxide based regimen.

label: text
content: - APL differentiation syndrome:

label: text
content: Maintain a high index of suspicion of APL differentiation syndrome (ie, fever, often associated with increasing WBC count >10 x $1 0^{9}$ /L, usually at initial diagnosis or relapse; shortness of breath; hypoxemia; pleural or pericardial effusions). $^{2}$ Close monitoring of volume overload and pulmonary status is indicated. Initiate dexamethasone at first signs or symptoms of respiratory compromise (ie, hypoxemia, pulmonary infiltrates, pericardial or pleural effusions) (10 mg BID for 3-5 days with a taper over 2 weeks). Consider interrupting ATRA therapy until hypoxia resolves.

label: text
content: For patients at high risk (WBC count >10 x 10 $^{9}$ /L) for developing differentiation syndrome and for those receiving an ATRA/arsenic trioxide-based regimen, initiate prophylaxis with corticosteroids, either prednisone 0.5 mg/kg day 1 or dexamethasone 10 mg every 12 hours (see NCCN Guidelines for Prevention and Treatment of Cancer-Related Infections). Taper the steroid dose over a period of several days. If patient develops differentiation syndrome, change prednisone to dexamethasone 10 mg every 12 hours until count recovery or risk of differentiation has abated. $^{2,3}$

label: text
content: Hydroxyurea can be used to treat leukocytosis associated with differentiation syndrome. In difficult-to-treat cases, an anthracycline (daunorubicin or idarubicin) or gemtuzumab ozogamicin can be used.

label: text
content: - Arsenic trioxide monitoring:

label: text
content: Prior to initiating therapy

label: text
content: Electrocardiogram (ECG) for prolonged QTc interval assessment

label: text
content: $\diamond$ Serum electrolytes and creatinine

label: text
content: During therapy (weekly during induction therapy and before each course of post-remission therapy)

label: text
content: Minimize use of drugs that may prolong QT interval.

label: text
content: Maintain K and Mg concentrations within middle or upper range of normal.

label: text
content: $\diamond$ In patients with prolonged QTc interval >500 millisec, correct electrolytes and proceed with caution. QTcF is recommended; however, in settings where QTcF corrections are unavailable, a cardiology consult may be appropriate for patients with prolonged QTc. $^{4}$

label: text
content: - Myeloid growth factors should not be used during induction. They may be considered during consolidation in selected cases (ie, life-threatening infections, signs/symptoms of sepsis); however, there are no outcomes data regarding the prophylactic use of growth factors in consolidation.

label: text
content: $^{a}$ Antiviral prophylaxis against varicella-zoster virus for duration of treatment may be appropriate. Glass JL, et al. Blood 2015;126:3752.

label: footnote
content: $^{1}$ Daver N, et al. Br J Haematol 2015;168:646-653.

label: footnote
content: $^{2}$ Lo-Coco F, et al. N Engl J Med 2013;369:111-121.

label: footnote
content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

J Clin Oncol 2017;35:3021-3029. $^{b}$ The optimal dosing of arsenic trioxide for patients with obesity is unknown, though retrospective studies suggest there may be a role for dose capping. Zacholski K, et al. J Oncol Pharm Pract 2022;28:1340-1349; Jen WY, et al. Leuk Lymphoma 2024;65:378-382.

label: vision_footnote
content: $^{c}$ Begin prophylaxis with prednisone; the optimal duration of steroid prophylaxis is unknown. If differentiation syndrome develops, change to dexamethasone. Lo-Coco F, et al. N Engl J Med 2013;369:111-121. See Principles of Supportive Care for APL (APL-A).

label: footnote
content: $^{a}$ Data suggest that lower doses of ATRA (25 mg/m $^{2}$ ) in divided doses until clinical remission may be used in children and adolescents. Kutny MA, et al. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:3021-3029.

label: footnote
content: $^{b}$ The optimal dosing of arsenic trioxide for patients with obesity is unknown, though retrospective studies suggest there may be a role for dose capping. Zacholski K, et al. J Oncol Pharm Pract 2022;28:1340-1349; Jen WY, et al. Leuk Lymphoma 2024;65:378-382.

label: footnote
content: $^{d}$ Although the original regimen included high-dose cytarabine (HiDAC) as second consolidation, some investigators recommend using HiDAC early for CNS prophylaxis, especially for patients not receiving IT chemotherapy.

label: footnote
content: $^{e}$ Consider IT chemotherapy (eg, 2 doses for each consolidation cycle) as an option for CNS prophylaxis.

label: footnote
content: Additional gemtuzumab ozogamicin may be considered during consolidation if PCR remains positive beyond 3 months of consolidation.

content: ## PRINCIPLES OF SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR APL

label: reference
content:
FOOTNOTES FOR APL TREATMENT INDUCTION (HIGH RISK) IN PATIENTS WITH CARDIAC ISSUES
a Data suggest that lower doses of ATRA (25 mg/m $^{2}$ ) in divided doses until clinical remission may be used in children and adolescents. Kutny MA, et al. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:3021-3029.
b The optimal dosing of arsenic trioxide for patients with obesity is unknown, though retrospective studies suggest there may be a role for dose capping. Zacholski K, et al. J Oncol Pharm Pract 2022;28:1340-1349; Jen WY, et al. Leuk Lymphoma 2024;65:378-382.
d Although the original regimen included HiDAC as second consolidation, some investigators recommend using HiDAC early for CNS prophylaxis, especially for patients not receiving IT chemotherapy.
e Consider IT chemotherapy (eg, 2 doses for each consolidation cycle) as an option for CNS prophylaxis.
f Additional gemtuzumab ozogamicin may be considered during consolidation if PCR remains positive beyond 3 months of consolidation.
g Dose adjustment of cytarabine may be needed for patients >60 years or patients with renal dysfunction.
h Patients with high-risk disease who are >60 years did not receive cytarabine in consolidation and were treated in the intermediate-risk group in the LPA2005 study.

label: reference_content
content: $^{1}$ Mitoxantrone was reduced to 3 days in patients with intermediate-risk disease in the LPA2005 study.

content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

content:
TherapyRegimen
Gemtuzumab ozogamicin96mg/m2IV for a maximum of 6 doses
Arsenic trioxide±ATRA±gemtuzumab ozogamicin10Arsenic trioxide0.15mg/kg dailyj,k±ATRA45mg/m2in2 divided doses daily±a single dose of gemtuzumab ozogamicin until count recovery with BM confirmation of remission

label: footnote
content: $^{b}$ The optimal dosing of arsenic trioxide for patients with obesity is unknown, though retrospective studies suggest there may be a role for dose capping. Zacholski K, et al. J Oncol Pharm Pract 2022;28:1340-1349; Jen WY, et al. Leuk Lymphoma 2024;65:378-382.

label: footnote
content: Following the first cycle of consolidation, if the patient's disease is not in molecular remission (by quantitative PCR on BM sample), consider matched sibling or alternative donor (haploidentical, unrelated donor, or cord blood) HCT or clinical trial. Testing is recommended at least 2-3 weeks after the completion of arsenic trioxide to avoid false positives.

label: footnote
content: $^{k}$ Outcomes are uncertain in patients who received arsenic trioxide during initial induction/consolidation therapy.

label: vision_footnote
content: There is a small randomized trial that suggests that the addition of ATRA does not confer any benefit over arsenic trioxide alone. Raffoux E, et al. J Clin Oncol 2003;21:2326-2334.

label: image
content: The image is a flowchart titled "INTENSIVE INDUCTION ELIGIBLE" and "FAVORABLE/INTERMEDIATE-RISK GROUPS (AML-A)" with subheadings "TREATMENT INDUCTION", "Preferred", "Other Recommended", "Useful in Certain Circumstances", and "Follow-Up and Re-Induction After Cytarabine-Based Induction (AML-3)". The flowchart outlines steps for managing an intensive induction eligible AML by cytogenetics (core binding factor [CBF]-AML)c, Favorable-risk AML by molecular mutation profiled or intermediate-risk AML per ELN (AML-A), Favorable-risk AML by molecular mutation profiled or intermediate-risk AML per ELN (AML-A), AML with FLT3 mutation, Poor/adverse risk groups, and AML-2. Each step includes specific recommendations and actions to be taken, such as standardizing 7 + 3 daunorubicin or idarubicinj, gemtuzumab ozogamicink,i,o (CD33 positive)m, FLAG-IDAn (category 2B; use with caution in patients >60 y), FLAG-IDA n+ gemtuzumab ozogamicinl,o (CD33 positive)m (category 2B; use with caution in patients >60 y), CLAG-M (cladribine + cytarabine + G-CSF - mitoxantrone) (category 2B; use with caution in patients >60 y), Standard 7 + 3 daunorubicin or idarubicinj+ midostaurinp (FLT3-internal tandem duplication [ITD] or tyrosine kinase domain [TKD]) (category 1), Standard 7 + 3 daunorubicin or idarubicinj+ quizartinib (FLT3-ITD only) (category 1).

label: image
content:
The image is a flowchart titled "INTENSIVE INDUCTION ELIGIBLE" with three main sections: Poor/ADVERSE-RISK GROUPS (AML-A), Preferred, and Other Recommended.

In the first section, there are two subheadings:
- Therapy-related AML other than CBF-AML
- Antecedent MDS/chronic myelomonocytic leukemia (CMML)

Each subheading includes a list of specific conditions or treatments related to AML.

The second section is labeled "Preferred":
- CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal cytarabine and daunorubicinr (category 1, preferred for age ≥60 y)
- Standard 7 + 3 (daunorubicin or idarubicin)j (preferred for age <60 y)

The third section is labeled "Other Recommended":
- CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal cytarabine and daunorubicinr (for age <60 y)
- Standard 7 + 3 (daunorubicin or idarubicin)j (for age ≥60 y)
- Decitabine (days 1-5) + venetoclaxs,t,u
- Azacitidine + venetoclaxs,t,u
- CLAI (cladribine + idarubicin + cyatarabine) + venetoclax (category 2B)
- FLAG-IDAn + venetoclax (category 2B; use with caution in patients >60 y)

The final section is labeled "Follow-up and Re-Induction After Cytarabine-Based Induction (AML-3) or Follow-up After Induction Therapy with Lower Intensity Therapy (Intensive Induction Ineligible or Declines (AML-5))"

The flowchart outlines the treatment options based on the severity and type of AML, including therapy-related AML, predefined treatments, and other recommended treatments.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## FOOTNOTES FOR INTENSIVE INDUCTION ELIGIBLE

label: reference
content:
a Patients with elevated blast counts are at risk for tumor lysis and organ dysfunction secondary to leukocytosis. Measures to rapidly reduce the WBC count include leukapheresis, hydroxyurea, and/or a single dose of cytarabine. Prompt instruction of definitive therapy is essential.
b Poor performance/functional status and a comorbid medical condition, in addition to age, are factors that influence ability to tolerate standard induction therapy. Web-based tools available to evaluate the probability of CR and early death after standard induction therapy in patients aged ≥60 years with WML can be found at: Walter R席, et al. J Clin Oncol 2011;19:471-4423; Borenligh E, et al. J Geriatr Oncol 2011;12:550-556. Consider the use of geriatric assessment for patients with WML ≥60 years of age. Ritchie EK, et al. Blood Adv 2022;6:3812-3820; Min GJ, et al. Blood 2022;139:1648-1658; Saad M, et al. Blood 2020;136:2715-2719; Klepin HD, et al. Blood 2013;121:4287-4294. See NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology.
c Consider screening with FISH to identify translocations/abnormalities associated with CBF-AML.
d In-frame mutations in AML are more predictive of favorable outcomes than double mutations. Taube F, et al. Blood 2022;139:87-103; Wakita S, et al. Blood Adv 2022;6:238-247; Tafel K, et al. Blood 2021;138:137-147.
e Principles of Supportive Care for AML (AML-F).
f Monitoring During Therapy (AML-G).
g Consider referral to palliative care at the start of induction. El-Jawahri A, et al. JAMA Oncol 2021;7:238-245. See NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care.
h General Considerations and Supportive Care for patients with AML Who Prefer Not to Receive Blood Transfusions (AML-D).
i Principles of Systemic Therapy for AML (AML-E).
For patients who exceed anthracycline or have cardiac issues but are still able to receive intensive therapy, alternative non-anthracycline-containing regimens may be considered (eg, FLAG, clofarabine-regimens [category 3]). See Discussion.
k For CBF-AML with FLT3-TKD mutation, the Panel prefers gentuzumab ozogamicin over an FLT3 inhibitor. For CBF-AML with FLT3-ITD, there is insufficient data to recommend one as the primary treatment. If transplant is planned, note that prior studies have used a 60- to 90-day interval between the last administration of gentuzumab ozogamicin and HCT.
m Threshold for CD33 is not well-defined and may be ≥1% by flow cytometry.
n In times of drugabirate shortage, cladribine can be substituted for fludarubine.
o Gentuzumab ozogamicin may be beneficial in NPM1-mutated AML (Kapp-Schwoerer S, et al. Blood 2020;136:3041-3050). The role of gentuzumab ozogamicin in CEBPA-mutated AML is not established.
The RATIFY trial studied patients aged 18–60 years with FLT3-mutated AML. An extrapolation of the data suggests that patients aged 61–70 years with FLT3-mutated AML who are fit to receive T+3 should be offered midstaurant since it seems to provide a survival benefit without undue toxicity. Schert FR, et al. Blood 2019;133:840-846.
P Patients who receive transplant shortly before gentuzumab ozogamicin administration may be at risk for developing sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS). Wadleigh M, et al. Blood 2003;102:1578-1582. If transplant is planned, note that prior studies have used a 60- to 90-day interval between the last administration of gentuzumab ozogamicin and HCT.
m Threshold for CD33 is not well-defined and may be ≥1% by flow cytometry.
n In times of drugabirate shortage, cladribine can be substituted for fludarubine.
o Gentuzumab ozogamicin may be beneficial in NPM1-mutated AML (Kapp-Schwoerer S, et al. Blood 2020;136:3041-3050). The role of gentuzumab ozogamicin in CEBPA-mutated AML is not established.
The RATIFY trial studied patients aged 18–60 years with FLT3-mutated AML. For patients with WML with cytogenetic changes consistent with MDS (previously classified as AML-MRC) and previous hypomethylating agent (HMA) exposure, the benefit from standard induction did not differ from the benefit with CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cataractine and daunorubicin. Lancet JE, et al. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:2684-2692. While the mutational definition of AML-MRC as it applies to the use of CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal cataractine and daunorubicin was not in the original trial, it can be considered. There is emerging data that CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal cataractine and daunorubicin provides most benefit for patients with WML with mutations in SRSF2, SF3B1, EZH2, U2A41, ZRRS2, BCOR, STAG2, or ASX1.1 (Shimony SO, et al. Blood 2024;144:60).
Venetokinase combination regimens may be continued for patients whose disease demonstrates clinical improvement (CR/CR with incomplete hematologic damage [CRI]), with consideration of subsequent transplants. DiNARCO CD, et al. Lennon OCD, 1919;126-228; Weil A, et al. Blood 2017;130:89. DiNARCO CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17.
P Patients whose disease has progressed to AML from MDS after significant exposure to HMAs (ie, azacitidine, decitabine) may be less likely to derive benefit from continued treatment with HMAs compared to patients who are HMA-naira. Alternative treatment strategies should be considered. DiNARCO CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17.
u Patients with cytopenias with disease in remission should take breaks between cycles. For more details about cycle length, see Venetokinase (AML-)

content:
Markdown: Consider follow-up BMf aspirate and biopsy 14-21 days after start of therapy w,xResidual disease (if ambiguous, repeat BM biopsy within 7 days before proceeding with therapy)RE-INDUCTION
• Cytarabine
• Standard 7 + 3 (daunorubicin or idarubicin)²
• 5 + 2 [(daunorubicin or idarubicin) or mitoxantrone for age ≥60 y]²
• Standard 7 + 3 (daunorubicin or idarubicin) + midostaurin² (FLT3 mutated [ITD or TKD])
• Standard 7 + 3 or 5 + 2 (daunorubicin or idarubicin) + quizartinib² (FLT3-ITD only)
• HMA (azacitidine or decitabine) + venetoclaxs,tu
• CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cytarabine and daunorubicin (therapy-related AML other than CBF-AML, antecedent MDS/CMLL, or cytogenetic or molecular changes consistent with MDS) (preferred only if given in induction; BM aspirate and biopsy 14-21 days after start of therapy)
• Consider regimens for relapsed/refractory disease, including targeted therapies, if lack of response to induction (Response criteria, see AML-1), Therapy for Relapsed/Refractory Disease (AML-9)HypoplasiayAwait recoveryBM aspirate and biopsy to document remission status upon count recovery, or by day 42 at the latest in the setting of delayed count recoveryaaResponse (Screening LP, see AML-B) (Response criteria, see AML-1)Consolidation (if received intensive re-induction) (AML-6)Lack of response to induction/Primary refractory (Response criteria, see AML-1)Continue lower intensity therapy (if received HMA + venetoclax re-induction) (AML-5)Allogeneic HCT (See NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation)Cytarabine (if not previously used as treatment for persistent disease at day 15) ± anthracycline (daunorubicin or idarubicin)² if a clinical trial is not available while awaiting identification of a donorTherapy for Relapsed/Refractory Disease (AML-9)Best supportive care

label: paragraph_title
content: ## FOOTNOTES FOR FOLLOW-UP AND REINDUCTION AFTER CYTARABINE-BASED INDUCTION

label: reference_content
content: f Monitoring During Therapy (AML-G).

label: reference
content:
i Principles of Systemic Therapy for AML (AML-E).
s Venetoclax combination regimens may be continued for patients whose disease demonstrates clinical improvement (CR/CR with incomplete hematologic recovery [CRi]), with consideration of subsequent transplant, where appropriate. DiNardo CD, et al. Lancet Oncol 2018;19:216-228; Wei A, et al. Blood 2017;130:890; DiNardo CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17; DiNardo CD, et al. N Engl J Med 2020;383:617-629; Kadia TM, et al. J Clin Oncol 2022;40:3848-3857.
t Patients whose disease has progressed to AML from MDS after significant exposure to HMAs (ie, azacitidine, decitabine) may be less likely to derive benefit from continued treatment with HMAs compared to patients who are HMA-naive. Alternative treatment strategies should be considered. DiNardo CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17.
u Patients with cytopenias with disease in remission should take breaks between cycles. For more details about cycle length, see Principles of Venetoclax (AML-J).
v Consider clinical trials for patients with disease with targeted molecular abnormalities.
w When using a cytarabine-based induction regimen with doses of cytarabine >100 to 200 mg/m $^{2}$ , consider delaying BM aspirate and biopsy to D21.
x There are limited prospective data to support this recommendation. Othus M, et al. Leukemia 2016;30:1779-1780.
y Hypoplasia is defined as cellularity <20% of which the residual blasts are <5% (ie, blast percentage of residual cellularity).
z For regimens using high cumulative doses of cardiotoxic agents, consider reassessing cardiac function prior to each anthracycline/mitoxantrone-containing course. Karanes C, et al. Leuk Res 1999;23:787-794.
aa When performed, BM aspirate and biopsy should include cytogenetic and molecular studies, as appropriate. For measurable (minimal) residual disease (MRD) assessment, see AML-H.

label: image
content:
The image is a flowchart detailing the treatment and selection process for lower intensity therapy (INTENSIVE INDUCTION INELIGIBLE OR DECLINES) in patients with IDH1 mutation.

At the top left, there is a heading "LOWER INTENSITY THERAPY (INTENSIVE INDUCTION INELIGIBLE OR DECLINES)" followed by a subheading "RISK GROUPS."

Below this, there are two main branches:
- The first branch is labeled "AML with IDH1 mutation."
- The second branch is labeled "AML without IDH1 mutation."

In both branches, there are several subheadings:
- "Preferred"
- "Useful in Certain Circumstances"
- "Low-dose cytarabine (LDAC) + venetoclax $^s$,u (prior exposure to HMA)"
- "Azacitidine or decitabine $^{t,ff}$ (contraindication to venetoclax)"
- "Olutasidenib $^{cc,dd}$ (category 2B) (not eligible for preferred regimen and not eligible for ivosidenib due to prolonged QTcF)

In the middle of the flowchart, there is a section titled "Follow-up After Induction Therapy with Lower Intensity Therapy (Intensive Induction Ineligible or Declines) (AML-5)."

This section appears to be a continuation of the flowchart, but it does not contain any text. It seems to be a placeholder or a summary line.

The flowchart itself is organized into three columns: Preferred, Useful in Certain Circumstances, and Other Recommended. Each column contains a list of specific treatments or regimens associated with the IDH1 mutation.

For example:
- **Preferred**:
  - Azacitidine + venetoclax (category 1)$^s$,u
  - Decitabine + venetoclax $^s$,u
  - Cladribine + LDAC + venetoclax $^s$ (category 2B)
  - LDAC + venetoclax $^s$,u (prior exposure to hypomethylating agent [HMA])
  - Azacitidine or decitabine $^{t,ff}$ (contraindication to venetoclax)
  - LDAC + glasdegib$$^g$$
  - LDAC (prior exposure to HMA or contraindication to venetoclax)
  - Gilteritinib ± azacitidine $^t$ (FLT3-ITD or TKD, not eligible for preferred regimen)
  - Enasidenib ± azacitidine $^{t,cc,dd}$ (IDH2 mutation, not eligible for preferred regimen)
  - Gemtuzumab ozogamicin$$^hh$ (CD33 positive, not eligible for preferred regimen)$$^m$$

The layout is clean and uses clear headings and bullet points to organize the information.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## FOOTNOTES FOR LOWER INTENSITY THERAPY (INTENSIVE INDUCTION INELIGIBLE OR DECLINES)

label: text
content: $^{a}$ Patients with elevated blast counts are at risk for tumor lysis and organ dysfunction secondary to leukostasis. Measures to rapidly reduce the WBC count include leukapheresis, hydroxyurea, and/or a single dose of cytarabine. Prompt institution of definitive therapy is essential.

label: text
content: b Poor performance/functional status and a comorbid medical condition, in addition to age, are factors that influence ability to tolerate standard induction therapy. Web-based tools available to evaluate the probability of CR and early death after standard induction therapy in patients aged $\geq60$ years with AML can be found at: Walter RB, et al. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:4417-4423; Borlenghi E, et al. J Geriatr Oncol 2021;12:550-556. Consider the use of geriatric assessment for patients with AML $\geq60$ years of age. Ritchie EK, et al. Blood Adv 2022;6:3812-3820; Min GJ, et al. Blood 2022;139:1646-1658; Saad M, et al. Blood 2020;136:2715-2719; Klepin H, et al. Blood 2013;121:4287-4294. See NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology.

label: text
content: e Principles of Supportive Care for AML (AML-F).

label: text
content: $^{g}$ Consider referral to palliative care for consultation at the start of induction. El-Jawahri A, et al. JAMA Oncol 2021;7:238-245. See NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care. $^{h}$ General Considerations and Supportive Care for Patients Who Prefer Not to Receive Blood Transfusions (AML-D).

label: text
content: Principles of Systemic Therapy for AML (AML-E).

label: text
content: m Threshold for CD33 is not well-defined and may be $\geq1\%$ by flow cytometry.

label: text
content: $^{s}$ Venetoclax combination regimens may be continued for patients whose disease demonstrates clinical improvement (CR/CRi), with consideration of subsequent transplant, where appropriate. DiNardo CD, et al. Lancet Oncol 2018;19:216-228; Wei A, et al. Blood 2017;130:890; DiNardo CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17; DiNardo CD, et al. N Engl J Med 2020;383:617-629. Kadia TM, et al. J Clin Oncol 2022;40:3848-3857.

label: text
content: Patients whose disease has progressed to AML from MDS after significant exposure to HMAs (ie, azacitidine, decitabine) may be less likely to derive benefit from continued treatment with HMAs compared to patients who are HMA-naive. Alternative treatment strategies should be considered. DiNardo CD, et al. Blood 2019;133:7-17.

label: text
content: Patients with cytopenias with disease in remission should take breaks between cycles. For more details about cycle length, see Principles of Venetoclax (AML-J).

label: text
content: $^{b b}$ For patients who decline induction chemotherapy and/or targeted therapy, best supportive care may include hydroxyurea and/or transfusion support.

label: text
content: Response to treatment with IDH inhibitors may take 3-5 months.

label: text
content: $^{d d}$ IDH inhibitors increase the risk for differentiation syndrome and hyperleukocytosis that may require treatment with hydroxyurea and steroids. Monitor closely for differentiation syndrome and initiate therapy to resolve symptoms according to indications. Note that differentiation syndrome can occur later (up to several months after induction).

label: text
content: This regimen is approved for patients with newly diagnosed AML with an IDH1 mutation who met at least one of the following criteria: aged >75 years, baseline Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 2, severe cardiac or pulmonary disease, hepatic impairment with bilirubin >1.5 times the upper limit of normal, creatinine clearance (CrCl) <45 mL/min, or other comorbidity. Montesinos P, et al. N Engl J Med 2022;386:1519-1531.

label: text
content: $^{f f}$ Response may not be evident before 3-4 cycles of treatment with HMAs (ie, azacitidine, decitabine). Continue HMA treatment until progression if patient is tolerating therapy. Similar delays in response are likely with novel agents in a clinical trial, but endpoints will be defined by the protocol.

label: text
content: 99 This regimen is for treatment of newly diagnosed AML in patients who are $\geq75$ years of age, or who have significant comorbid conditions (ie, severe cardiac disease, ECOG performance status $\geq2$ , baseline creatinine $>1.3$ mg/dL) and has been associated with an improved overall survival (OS) in a randomized trial. Cortes JE, et al. Leukemia 2019;33:379-389.

label: text
content: hh Regimens that include gemtuzumab ozogamicin have limited benefit in poor-risk disease.

content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

label: image
content:
The image is a flowchart titled "CONSOLIDATION THERAPY $^{ij,kk}$ RISK GROUP (AML-A)" and it outlines the treatment steps for intensive induction eligible patients.

1. **Favorable-risk AML by cytogenetics (CBF-AML) or by molecular mutation profile per ELN (AML-A)$^{d,ll}$**
   - Cytarabine $^{mm}$ ± gemtuzumab ozogamicin $^i$ (CD33 positive)$^m$ (only if gemtuzumab ozogamicin was given during induction)
   - Cytarabine $^{mm}$ (5 or 7 days) ± [(daunorubicin or idarubicin] or [mitoxantrone for age ≥60 y]$^z$
   - Cytarabine $^{mm}$ + (daunorubicin or idarubicin) + gemtuzumab ozogamicin $^{l,z,nn}$ (CD33 positive)$^m$ (only if gemtuzumab ozogamicin was given during induction)

2. **AML with FLT3 mutation**
   - Allogeneic HCT (preferred for FLT3-ITD)$^{jij,kk,oo}$
   - Cytarabine $^{mm}$ + midostaurin $^p$ (FLT3-ITD or TKD)
   - Cytarabine $^{mm}$ + quizartinib (FLT3-ITD only)

3. **Intermediate-risk AML**
   - Cytarabine $^{mm,pp}$
   - Cytarabine $^{mm}$ + (daunorubicin or idarubicin) + gemtuzumab ozogamicin $^{l,z,nn}$ (CD33 positive)$^m$ (only if gemtuzumab ozogamicin was given during induction)
   - Allogeneic HCT $^{ji,jk}$

4. **Poor-risk AML with and without TP53 mutation or del(17p) abnormality**
   - Allogeneic HCT $^{ji,jk}$ (preferred)
   - Cytarabine $^{mm}$
   - CGX-351 (dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cytarabine and daunorubicin $^f$ (preferred only if given during induction)
   - FLAG-DA (use with caution in patients >60 y) (preferred only if given during induction)$^n,mm$
   - Continuation of lower intensity regimen used for induction (eg, HMA, [azacitidine or decitabine] + venetoclax) $(AML-4)$

5. **Antecodent MDS/CMML**
6. **Cytogenetic changes consistent with MDS (previously classified as AML-MRC)**

The chart also includes considerations for allogeneic HCT $^{i}$, Maintenance (AML-7), Surveillance (AML-8).

content: Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

label: paragraph_title
content: ## FOOTNOTES FOR CONSOLIDATION THERAPY (INTENSIVE INDUCTION ELIGIBLE)

label: reference
content:
d In-frame b2IP mutations in CEBPA are more predictive of favorable outcomes than double mutations. Taube F, et al. Blood 2022;139:87-103; Wakita S, et al. Blood Adv 2022;6:238-247; Tarkook K, et al. Blood 2021;138:1137-1147.

i Principles of Systemic Therapy for AML (AML-E).

i Patients who receive transplant shortly following gemtuzumab ozogamicin administration may be at risk for developing SOS. Wadleigh M, et al. Blood 2003;102:1578-1582. If transplant is planned, note that prior studies have used a 60- to 90-day interval between the last administration of gemtuzumab ozogamicin and HCT.

m Threshold for CD33 is not well-defined and may be $\geq1\%$ by flow cytometry.

n In times of fludarabine shortage, cladribine can be substituted for fluudarabine.

p The RATIFY trial studied patients aged 18–60 years with FLT3-mutated AML. An extrapolation of the data suggests that patients aged 61–70 years with FLT3-mutated AML who are fit to receive 7+3 should be offered midstaurin since it seems to provide a survival benefit without undue toxicity. Schlenk RF, et al. Blood 2019;133:840-851.

r There are limited data supporting the use of this regimen in patients aged <60 years. For patients with AML with cytogenetic changes consistent with MDS (previously classified as AML-MRC) and previous HMA exposure, the benefit from standard induction did not differ from the benefit with CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cytarabine and daunorubicin. Lancet JE, et al. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:2684-2692. While the mutational definition of AML-MRC as it applies to the use of CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal cytarabine and daunorubicin was not studied in the original trial, its use can be considered. There is emerging data that CPX-351/dual-drug liposomal encapsulation of cytarabine and daunorubicin provides most benefit for patients with AML with mutations in SRSF2, SF3B1, EZH2, U2AF1, ZRSR2, BCOR, STAG2, or ASXL1 (Shimmy SO, et al. Blood 2024;144:60).

z For regimens using high cumulative doses of cardiotoxic agents, consider reassessing cardiac function prior to each anthracycline/mitoxantrone-containing course. Karanes C, et al. Leuk Res 1999;23:787-794.

jj For appropriate patients, begin alternate donor search (haploidentical, unrelated donor, or cord blood) if no appropriate matched sibling donor is available and the patient is a candidate for allogeneic HCT. For lack of response to induction, alternative therapy to achieve remission is encouraged prior to HCT. See NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation.

kk Patients eligible for allogeneic HCT may require at least one cycle of consolidation while donor search is in progress or while awaiting collaboration with a transplant center to maintain remission. Patients may proceed directly to transplant following achievement of remission if a donor is available.

li See Measurable (Minimal) Residual Disease Assessment (AML-H) for additional guidance on MRD monitoring for patients with CBF-AML and NPM1-mutated AML.

mm Alternate dosing of cytarabine for postremission therapy has been reported (Discussion). Jaramillo S, et al. Blood Cancer J 2017;7:e564. Doses of cytarabine $\geq2$ g/m $^2$ should be used with caution in patients $\geq60$ years and patients with renal failure due to concern for neurotoxicity. See Principles of Systemic Therapy for AML (AML-E).

mn This regimen may also be used in patients with AML with KIT mutations because the outcomes are similar in patients with AML without KIT mutations.

oo Consider NPM1 molecular MRD status, if applicable.

pp There is no evidence that cytarabine doses $\geq2$ g/m $^2$ are superior to doses 1-2 g/m $^2$ in patients with AML with intermediate-risk cytogenetics.

qq Allogeneic transplant is recommended for patients with favorable-risk disease who are unable to complete consolidation or who have high-risk features such as MRD-positionity or KIT mutation.

Markdown: MAINTENANCE THERAPYTREATMENT¹Post Chemotherapy• Patient with non-CBF-AML:
► Received prior intensive chemotherapy and disease is now in remission
► Completed no consolidation or some consolidation
► No allogeneic HCT is planned• Consider oral azacitidine until progression or unacceptable toxicity (category 1 for age ≥55 y)tt,uu
• Useful in Certain Circumstances:
► Azacitidine or decitabine for a maximum of 12 cycles (unable to receive oral azacitidine)• Patient with history of FLT3 mutation:
► Previously received FLT3 inhibitor
► No allogeneic HCT is planned• Quizartinib (FLT3-ITD only) (preferred for FLT3-ITD)
• Midostaurin (FLT3-ITD or TKD)Post Allogeneic HCTPost allogeneic HCT, in remission, and history of FLT3 mutation• Gilteritinib (FLT3-ITD or TKD) (preferred for FLT3-ITD in CR1 without MRD negativity by ultrasensitive assay pre-transplant)
• Sorafenib (FLT3-ITD only)
• Midostaurin (FLT3-ITD or TKD)
• Quizartinib (FLT3-ITD only)Post allogeneic HCT, in remission, with history of AML with high-risk features™,ss• Low-dose decitabine + G-CSF (category 2B)

label: footnote
content: $^{i}$ Principles of Systemic Therapy for AML (AML-E).

label: footnote
content: ELN Risk Stratification by Biological Disease

label: footnote
content: Factors for Patients with Non-APL AML Treated with Intensive Induction Chemotherapy (AML-A).

label: footnote
content: $^{ss}$ Gao L, et al. J Clin Oncol 2020;38:4249-4259.

label: footnote
content: $^{t t}$ This is not intended to replace consolidation chemotherapy. In addition, patients who are fit may benefit from HCT in first CR, and there are no data to suggest that maintenance therapy with oral azacitidine can replace HCT. The Panel also notes that the trial did not include patients <55 years of age or those with CBF-AML; it was restricted to patients $\geq55$ years of age with AML with intermediate or adverse cytogenetics who were not felt to be candidates for HCT. Most patients received at least 1 cycle of consolidation prior to starting oral azacitidine. Wei AH, et al. N Engl J Med 2020;383:2526-2537.

label: footnote
content: There are certain circumstances where oral azacitidine may be of benefit for those who have completed a recommended course of consolidation. Dohner H, et al. Blood 2022;140:1674-1685.
